content.problemStatement:
Chef is fascinated by number sequences and discovered a special sequence called the **Count-and-Say Sequence**.

The sequence is defined as follows:


The **first term** is `"1"`.
Each subsequent term is generated by applying **run-length encoding (RLE)** to the previous term:

For each group of consecutive identical digits, write the **count** followed by the **digit**.


For example:


`"1"` → `"11"` (one `1`)
`"11"` → `"21"` (two `1`s)
`"21"` → `"1211"` (one `2`, one `1`)
You are given an integer nnn. Your task is to generate the **n-th term** of the sequence.

Note:Solve the problem **iteratively** to avoid stack overflow for larger values of nnn.

Function DeclarationFunction NamecountAndSaycountAndSaycountAndSay – This function generates the **n-th term** of the **Count-and-Say Sequence** using an **iterative approach**.

Parameters
nnn : A positive integer representing the position in the Count-and-Say sequence.
Return Value
Returns a string representing the **n-th term** of the Count-and-Say Sequence.
Constraints
1≤T≤501 \leq T \leq 501≤T≤50
1≤n≤301 \leq n \leq 301≤n≤30

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each of the next TTT lines contains a single integer nnn.


Output Format

For each test case, print a single line containing the **n-th term** of the Count-and-Say Sequence.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 50), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 30) – the position in the Count‑and‑Say sequence.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the n‑th term of the Count‑and‑Say sequence.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Applying the rule three times starting from "1" yields the 4th term "1211".

solution.approach: The sequence is generated iteratively. Start with the string "1". For each step from 2 to n, scan the current string, count consecutive identical digits and append count followed by the digit to build the next string. This is exactly a run‑length encoding of the previous term. Because n ≤ 30 the length of the strings stays well within memory limits, and an iterative loop avoids recursion depth problems.
solution.timeComplexity: O(L) per term where L is the length of the current term. Over all steps up to n the total work is O(total length of all generated strings), which for n ≤ 30 is negligible (≈ a few thousand operations).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) for storing the current term, where L is the length of the n‑th term (at most a few hundred characters).
